ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302757 covers fried tofu as a food product category; no exact entry for Thai-style golden crispy tofu exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried soybean curd broadly; no Thai-specific crispy tofu entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for fried tofu as a food item in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of golden crispy tofu is approximately 150 g, equivalent to 6–8 bite-sized pieces as served in a Thai restaurant or as a starter portion.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174292)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for fried tofu (FDC ID 174292) used as the primary nutrient basis. Values represent plain deep-fried firm tofu without sauce. Thai-style golden crispy tofu is closely analogous; minor coating (cornstarch/rice flour) may slightly elevate carbohydrate and energy values. Values adopted as a conservative estimate. No Singapore HPB Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for this specific preparation was identified.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Thai golden crispy tofu was found. GI of 48 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 entry for fried tofu / soy-based foods, which report GI values in the range 40–55 for plain fried tofu. A midpoint value of 48 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 13.05) / 100 ≈ 6.3, based on 150 g serving containing approximately 13.05 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content in plain fried tofu
hypertension.guidance: Plain golden crispy tofu contains approximately 13 mg sodium per 100 g, making it a low-sodium protein option; however, patients with hypertension should monitor dipping sauces (e.g., sweet chilli, soy-based) which can substantially increase sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 48 and a glycemic load of about 6.3 per 150 g serving, golden crispy tofu has a low glycaemic impact and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and caloric density; soy protein is cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying elevates total fat to approximately 19.5 g per 100 g, but the fat profile is predominantly polyunsaturated (11 g per 100 g) and soy protein has recognised LDL-lowering properties; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and choose lighter cooking oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden Crispy Tofu (Thai: เต้าหู้ทอดกรอบ, romanized: Tao Hu Thot Krob) is a popular Thai dish consisting of firm tofu that is cut into bite-sized cubes, lightly coated in cornstarch or rice flour, and deep-fried in vegetable oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crunchy while the interior remains soft and silky. The dish is a staple street food and restaurant starter across Thailand and is widely served in Thai restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The texture contrast between the crisp outer shell and the tender soy curd interior is the defining characteristic of the dish. It is typically served with a sweet chilli dipping sauce, crushed peanuts, or a light soy-vinegar dip. Nutritionally, golden crispy tofu is a moderate-energy food (approximately 271 kcal per 100 g) that is rich in plant-based protein (approximately 17 g per 100 g) and provides notable amounts of calcium (372 mg per 100 g) and iron (3 mg per 100 g) from the soy curd base. The fat content is elevated by deep-frying (approximately 19.5 g per 100 g) but is predominantly polyunsaturated, which is favourable for cardiovascular health. The glycemic index is low (approximately 48), making it suitable for glycaemic management when consumed without high-sugar accompaniments. It is naturally free of cholesterol and dairy, and is suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. The dish belongs to the soy-based protein food subgroup within the meat and alternatives food group.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai golden crispy tofu was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried tofu (FDC ID 174292), which is the closest approved-source match. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for soy-based fried foods. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 174292 (fried tofu). The Thai-style preparation with a light cornstarch or rice flour coating may slightly increase carbohydrate and energy values compared to the source entry; values are treated as a conservative estimate. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for this food type. vitamin_b12_mcg is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero as tofu is a plant-based food containing no vitamin B12 by nature (naturally zero). cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero as tofu is a plant-based food (naturally zero). 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae and vitamin_d_mcg and vitamin_e_mg are reported as -1 as these values are not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for fried tofu (FDC ID 174292) and cannot be reliably estimated without an approved source. 4. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables for fried soy-based foods; no direct measurement for this specific Thai preparation was found. 5. Sodium value of 13 mg per 100 g reflects plain fried tofu without added sauce; actual sodium intake may be significantly higher if dipping sauces are consumed. 6. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional menu title 'Golden Crispy Tofu Delight' to the canonical English name 'Golden Crispy Tofu'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302757 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology code for Thai-style golden crispy tofu exists in either system.

Iteration 1: Added GI analogous estimate (48, low) from Atkinson et al. 2021 / University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation.
Iteration 1: Added defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes as closest_parent with gap notes; SNOMED CT set to null/none with gap note.
Iteration 1: Added all three required health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.

Iteration 1: Documented all confirmed-zero values (trans_fat_g, vitamin_b12_mcg, cholesterol_mg) with 'confirmed zero' and 'naturally zero' phrases in notes.
